key_insight: Combining copy-number and fragmentomic cfDNA features from ultra-low coverage whole-genome sequencing produced a stronger ovarian cancer detection model than either feature type alone.
clinical_relevance: This integrative blood-based approach may support earlier, more scalable ovarian cancer screening, though it is still a research-stage test rather than a clinical standard.
method: Plasma cfDNA from 85 ovarian cancer patients and 41 cancer-free controls was sequenced at ~1x ulcWGS.
Researchers extracted 21 CNV and fragmentomic features and trained XGBoost models with hyperparameter optimization.
A dual-threshold strategy was used to separate confident calls from uncertain cases.
evidence_signal: On an independent test set, the combined model achieved AUC 0.900 with 85% sensitivity and 90% specificity, outperforming CNV-only and fragmentomics-only models.
watch_item: Results come from a relatively small cohort and require larger external validation before routine screening use.
paper_title: Integrative analysis of cfDNA features from ultra-low coverage whole genome sequencing enables robust detection of ovarian cancer
